title: The Meryl Streep Hall of Fame
format: Sean and Amanda open on the Cannes lineup and a run of release-date news, then build a Hall of Fame for Meryl Streep ahead of The Devil Wears Prada 2. They walk all 65 features film by film and induct ten, with a 'blue' apiece for an underseen one worth seeking out.

segments.heading: The Hall of Fame
segments.note: the ten inductees, chosen from 65 features

films.title: Kramer vs. Kramer
films.note: Her supporting Oscar. She pushed Robert Benton to rewrite the part more sympathetically to the mother, and won that fight. The controlled courtroom testimony, played without trying to win the audience over, is Sean's 'automatic green.'

films.title: Sophie's Choice
films.note: Her first Best Actress Oscar, five years into her film career. Immaculate Polish and German accent work, acting in other languages in the flashbacks. Neither host loves the film; both call the performance undeniable.

films.title: Silkwood
films.note: Mike Nichols' whistleblower drama, still not on Blu-ray. The green they pull out of her pure-drama 80s run, which they argue is her best work. A plain American woman opposite Cher and Kurt Russell.

films.title: A Cry in the Dark
films.note: The 'a dingo's got my baby' movie, though almost no one has seen it. A cold, prickly, unlikable turn as a real mother wrongly convicted, inside a sharp film about a media feeding frenzy.

films.title: Postcards from the Edge
films.note: Her third Mike Nichols film, a thinly veiled Carrie Fisher. The comedy-and-singing green, and the closest thing to Streep's real voice; she took it partly to face her fear of singing in public.

films.title: The Bridges of Madison County
films.note: Clint Eastwood directs and co-stars; Streep is an Italian war bride in Iowa. The framing device is dire, but the two leads are 'never better,' and the car scene at the end wrecked both hosts.

films.title: Adaptation
films.note: Charlie Kaufman and Spike Jonze; Streep as a fictionalized Susan Orlean who starts normal and comes apart by the end. The green for her willingness to get weird in the second half of her career.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada
films.note: Every power move played small and at a whisper, partly modeled on Clint Eastwood. Put her back at the center of the box office and introduced her to a new generation.

films.title: Mamma Mia!
films.note: In on the strength of what it made. 'The Winner Takes It All' sung up to Pierce Brosnan; she is genuinely likable and, both agree, not the problem with a strange movie.

films.title: Julie & Julia
films.note: Julia Child, already an SNL bit before this. Sean's case: she should have won for this instead of The Iron Lady. Joy and over-the-topness with the regret underneath, opposite Stanley Tucci.

segments.heading: The whole filmography, film by film
segments.note: every other Streep feature, in order: the yellows they nearly took, the reds they didn't, and the two 'blues' they told you to seek out (Heartburn, Ricki and the Flash)

films.title: Julia
films.note: Her one-scene debut, in a flashback opposite Jane Fonda. The one time you can see her acting, they agree: accent and costume not yet fused.

films.title: The Deer Hunter
films.note: Yellow. The announcement. An underwritten role she fills with gravitas and sadness, made while she was caring for the dying John Cazale, also in the film.

films.title: Manhattan
films.note: Red. A blistering, ball-breaking ex-wife on the outskirts of the film. Very good; not in.

films.title: The Seduction of Joe Tynan
films.note: Red. A Southern accent opposite Alan Alda's senator. She said she was on 'automatic pilot,' having taken it right after Cazale's death.

films.title: The French Lieutenant's Woman
films.note: Yellow. Two roles and an English accent, but it's Jeremy Irons' movie; the black-cloak-on-the-pier image outlasts the film.

films.title: Still of the Night
films.note: Red. A Robert Benton Hitchcock homage; she's the Tippi Hedren blonde. Slow and talky, the opposite of a real thriller.

films.title: Falling in Love
films.note: Red. With De Niro, commuters having an affair. Inert and much maligned, though Sean was quietly charmed by it.

films.title: Plenty
films.note: Red. Fred Schepisi; a postwar Englishwoman coming apart. Worth it for a great McKellen scene and Streep's fearlessness with an unlikable part.

films.title: Out of Africa
films.note: Red. A Best Picture winner both hosts find boring and airless. 'Like eating a rice cake with no water.'

films.title: Heartburn
films.note: BLUE. Nichols and Nora Ephron, opposite Jack Nicholson. Sexy and confessional, the blueprint for the Nancy Meyers movies Amanda loves. Underrated; seek it out.

films.title: Ironweed
films.note: Red. Hector Babenco's Depression drama; she and Nicholson as drifting drunks. Excruciatingly bleak, but her 'He's Me Pal' bar song is one of Sean's favorite Streep scenes.

films.title: She-Devil
films.note: Red. A funny camp turn as a romance novelist, but the movie pits her glamour against Roseanne Barr in a way that insults everyone.

films.title: Defending Your Life
films.note: Yellow. Albert Brooks' afterlife comedy; she's luminous and loose as the 'afterlife girl,' even if the part is thin.

films.title: Death Becomes Her
films.note: Yellow. Zemeckis' effects satire, a sharper She-Devil. A cult favorite; Sean finds it shrill, but it honors a lot of childhoods.

films.title: The House of the Spirits
films.note: Red. The Isabel Allende adaptation, a stacked cast with no Latin leads. A well-meaning, slow fiasco where everyone is miscast.

films.title: The River Wild
films.note: Yellow. Curtis Hanson thriller and the first Streep movie both hosts saw. Very physical, shot on the water; a genuine swerve for her.

films.title: Before and After
films.note: Red. A mother reckoning with a son's possible crime; a silly, forgettable story, though Molina's one scene entertains.

films.title: Marvin's Room
films.note: Red. Reunited with Diane Keaton, a young Leo in the mix. The mushy soft-middle of her career; fine, not much more.

films.title: Dancing at Lughnasa
films.note: Red. Brian Friel's five-sisters play in Ireland. A small, fine ensemble piece; her part is modest.

films.title: One True Thing
films.note: Red. She's the mother dying of cancer opposite Renée Zellweger and William Hurt. Affecting in the sick scenes; the film doesn't hold up.

films.title: Music of the Heart
films.note: Red. Wes Craven's only non-horror film, a bland Miramax teacher drama. Fun to clock the real violinists at the Carnegie Hall finale.

films.title: A.I. Artificial Intelligence
films.note: Red. A tiny voice role as Blue Mecha in a film they call a masterpiece; not a Streep Hall of Fame entry.

films.title: The Hours
films.note: Red. Neither host cares for it and neither revisited it.

films.title: The Manchurian Candidate
films.note: Red, but the pivot. A hard-bitten senator; her first real villain and the image both reach for when they think of late-period 'mean elder Meryl.'

films.title: Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events
films.note: Red. The nervous Aunt Josephine, made because her kids liked the books.

films.title: Prime
films.note: Red. A therapist whose patient dates her son (Uma Thurman, Bryan Greenberg). Perfectly fine; a road test for the Big Little Lies styling.

films.title: A Prairie Home Companion
films.note: Red. Altman's final film, shadow-directed by PTA. She's good and sings a lot in the ensemble; Lindsay Lohan plays her daughter.

films.title: The Ant Bully
films.note: Red. A voice role.

films.title: Dark Matter
films.note: Red. Quite possibly her least-seen film; unnecessary.

films.title: Evening
films.note: Red. A remarkable cast (Redgrave, Danes) across two timelines, and totally forgotten.

films.title: Rendition
films.note: Red. She oversees the CIA in a critical-but-unsuccessful political thriller; where Gyllenhaal and Witherspoon met.

films.title: Lions for Lambs
films.note: Red. Redford directs Cruise and Streep as a Judith Miller stand-in. On paper great; very bad.

films.title: Doubt
films.note: Yellow, with a case for green. John Patrick Shanley; some of the best work of Streep, Philip Seymour Hoffman and Viola Davis. A tough, painful watch.

films.title: Fantastic Mr. Fox
films.note: Red. A great voice turn, but it went in the Clooney Hall of Fame.

films.title: It's Complicated
films.note: Yellow. The under-celebrated Nancy Meyers, divorced from Alec Baldwin, an affair and a dream kitchen. Amanda loves it; a great hang.

films.title: The Iron Lady
films.note: Red. Margaret Thatcher, and the win Sean wishes had gone to Julie & Julia instead: 'a ridiculous Harvey Weinstein Oscar.' Phyllida Lloyd directs.

films.title: Hope Springs
films.note: Red. David Frankel; a long-married couple in therapy with Steve Carell. Folksy and forgettable.

films.title: August: Osage County
films.note: Red, but very watchable. Tracy Letts' play, poorly directed by John Wells; a big, showy Violet Weston with real vulnerability underneath.

films.title: The Giver
films.note: Red. The Chief Elder in the dystopian adaptation; she's fine.

films.title: The Homesman
films.note: Red. A Tommy Lee Jones western; a small part, and the one film neither host had seen.

films.title: Into the Woods
films.note: Red. She's the Witch in Rob Marshall's Sondheim, which neither host rates.

films.title: Ricki and the Flash
films.note: BLUE. Jonathan Demme's final feature; Streep as an aging rock star good at the rocking, bad at the family stuff. Sean's blue, barely seen, and he really enjoys it.

films.title: Suffragette
films.note: Red. A supporting turn as Emmeline Pankhurst.

films.title: Florence Foster Jenkins
films.note: Red. Oscar-nominated for a rich woman who can't sing and thinks she can. An SNL sketch stretched to a feature.

films.title: The Post
films.note: Red. A very good Katharine Graham in a Spielberg film that overstates the Post's role in the Pentagon Papers.

films.title: Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again
films.note: Red. She has far less to do, with Lily James carrying young Donna in flashback.

films.title: Mary Poppins Returns
films.note: Red. Another Rob Marshall; she's the magical Topsy. 'Wicked before Wicked.'

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: Red. A Soderbergh Panama Papers film they defended in 2019; she plays three roles, one an uncomfortable bit of cultural appropriation.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Red. A small, very funny Aunt March; a supporting turn that helps certify the film's importance.

films.title: Let Them All Talk
films.note: Red. Another Soderbergh; a novelist on a transatlantic crossing with two old friends. Amanda's near-blue; probably too small to induct.

films.title: The Prom
films.note: Red. Ryan Murphy. Very bad; makes both hosts angry.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: Red. Adam McKay's last film; she's a glorious-asshole US president. Fun, not a green.

films.title: Hoppers
films.note: Red. A good voice turn as the queen butterfly in the animated film; not in.

films.title: Project Hail Mary
films.note: Red. A vanishing voice cameo as Rocky.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada 2
films.note: The reason for the episode, and unseen at recording: her first true lead since Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again.

films: Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma

Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate.title: Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events

Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again.title: Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again

Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma.title: Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma
